subject: Introduction to Supervised Learning
main_goal: Explain how supervised learning uses labeled data, how models are trained, and why evaluation matters.

key_topics.name: Features and labels
key_topics.description: Each training example contains features, which are the inputs, and a label, which is the expected output.

key_topics.name: Training and validation split
key_topics.description: The training split is used to fit the model, while the validation split helps estimate how well the model generalizes.

key_topics.name: Loss functions
key_topics.description: Loss functions measure the difference between predictions and true labels.

key_topics.name: Gradient descent
key_topics.description: Gradient descent is a common optimization algorithm that updates model parameters step by step in the direction that reduces loss.

key_topics.name: Overfitting and regularization
key_topics.description: Regularization techniques, simpler models, and better validation practices can improve generalization.

summary: Supervised learning is a machine learning approach where a model learns from input-output pairs. Each training example contains features, which are the inputs, and a label, which is the expected output. The model studies patterns in the data so that it can make predictions for new examples. The dataset is usually divided into training and validation data.
key_points: Explain how supervised learning uses labeled data, how models are trained, and why evaluation matters.
Each training example contains features, which are the inputs, and a label, which is the expected output.
The training split is used to fit the model, while the validation split helps estimate how well the model generalizes.
Loss functions measure the difference between predictions and true labels.
Gradient descent is a common optimization algorithm that updates model parameters step by step in the direction that reduces loss.
